text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pure Vowels}} is defined as: Italian vowels are never reduced to a schwa. They remain clear even in unstressed syllables.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Pure Vowels
Description: Italian vowels are never reduced to a schwa. They remain clear even in unstressed syllables.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Pure Vowels' es: Italian vowels are never reduced to a schwa. They remain clear even in unstressed syllables.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Mid-Vowel Contrast}} is defined as: There is a phonemic distinction between /e/ vs. /ɛ/ and /o/ vs. /ɔ/ (e.g., *pésche* "fishing" vs. *pèsche* "peaches"), though this varies by region.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Mid-Vowel Contrast
Description: There is a phonemic distinction between /e/ vs. /ɛ/ and /o/ vs. /ɔ/ (e.g., *pésche* "fishing" vs. *pèsche* "peaches"), though this varies by region.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Mid-Vowel Contrast' es: There is a phonemic distinction between /e/ vs. /ɛ/ and /o/ vs. /ɔ/ (e.g., *pésche* "fishing" vs. *pèsche* "peaches"), though this varies by region.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Diphthongs}} is defined as: Common combinations like /ai/, /au/, /ei/, /eu/.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Diphthongs' es: Common combinations like /ai/, /au/, /ei/, /eu/.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Gemination}} is defined as: Double consonants are significantly longer and change the word's meaning (e.g., *pala* "shovel" vs. *palla* "ball").
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Gemination
Description: Double consonants are significantly longer and change the word's meaning (e.g., *pala* "shovel" vs. *palla* "ball").
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Gemination' es: Double consonants are significantly longer and change the word's meaning (e.g., *pala* "shovel" vs. *palla* "ball").

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Palatal Sounds}} is defined as: Italian features common palatal sounds like /ɲ/ (written *gn*) and /ʎ/ (written *gli*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Palatal Sounds
Description: Italian features common palatal sounds like /ɲ/ (written *gn*) and /ʎ/ (written *gli*).
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Palatal Sounds' es: Italian features common palatal sounds like /ɲ/ (written *gn*) and /ʎ/ (written *gli*).

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico}} is defined as: A rule where the initial consonant of a word is doubled in pronunciation after certain words ending in a stressed vowel, monosyllables, or specific structural words:
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico
Description: A rule where the initial consonant of a word is doubled in pronunciation after certain words ending in a stressed vowel, monosyllables, or specific structural words:
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico' es: A rule where the initial consonant of a word is doubled in pronunciation after certain words ending in a stressed vowel, monosyllables, or specific structural words:

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Elision}} is defined as: Dropping the final unstressed vowel of a word before another word starting with a vowel, marked with an apostrophe (e.g., *l'amico* instead of *lo amico*, *quest'anno* instead of *questo anno*, *un'ora* instead of *una ora*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Elision
Description: Dropping the final unstressed vowel of a word before another word starting with a vowel, marked with an apostrophe (e.g., *l'amico* instead of *lo amico*, *quest'anno* instead of *questo anno*, *un'ora* instead of *una ora*).
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Elision' es: Dropping the final unstressed vowel of a word before another word starting with a vowel, marked with an apostrophe (e.g., *l'amico* instead of *lo amico*, *quest'anno* instead of *questo anno*, *un'ora* instead of *una ora*).

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Apocope (Troncamento)}} is defined as: Dropping the final vowel or syllable before consonant-initial words under specific conditions, without an apostrophe (e.g., *buon giorno* instead of *buono giorno*, *parlar chiaro* instead of *parlare chiaro*, *dottor Rossi* instead of *dottore Rossi*).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Apocope (Troncamento)
Description: Dropping the final vowel or syllable before consonant-initial words under specific conditions, without an apostrophe (e.g., *buon giorno* instead of *buono giorno*, *parlar chiaro* instead of *parlare chiaro*, *dottor Rossi* instead of *dottore Rossi*).
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Apocope (Troncamento)' es: Dropping the final vowel or syllable before consonant-initial words under specific conditions, without an apostrophe (e.g., *buon giorno* instead of *buono giorno*, *parlar chiaro* instead of *parlare chiaro*, *dottor Rossi* instead of *dottore Rossi*).

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Syllable-timed Rhythm}} is defined as: Maintain even tempo on each syllable. Do not reduce vowels to a schwa [ə]; even unstressed final vowels must remain pure and fully voiced.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Syllable-timed Rhythm
Description: Maintain even tempo on each syllable. Do not reduce vowels to a schwa [ə]; even unstressed final vowels must remain pure and fully voiced.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Syllable-timed Rhythm' es: Maintain even tempo on each syllable. Do not reduce vowels to a schwa [ə]; even unstressed final vowels must remain pure and fully voiced.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Word Blending}} is defined as: In rapid spoken speech, vowel sequences between adjacent words are glided together rather than separated by pauses (e.g., *come era* -> "com'era" [koˈmɛːra], *dove sei* -> "do' sei" [doˈsɛj] in informal regional accents).
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Word Blending
Description: In rapid spoken speech, vowel sequences between adjacent words are glided together rather than separated by pauses (e.g., *come era* -> "com'era" [koˈmɛːra], *dove sei* -> "do' sei" [doˈsɛj] in informal regional accents).
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Word Blending' es: In rapid spoken speech, vowel sequences between adjacent words are glided together rather than separated by pauses (e.g., *come era* -> "com'era" [koˈmɛːra], *dove sei* -> "do' sei" [doˈsɛj] in informal regional accents).

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Word Stress}} is defined as: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable (**Paro**-la). Words stressed on the last syllable (*Città*) always have a written accent.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Word Stress
Description: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable (**Paro**-la). Words stressed on the last syllable (*Città*) always have a written accent.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Word Stress' es: Usually falls on the penultimate syllable (**Paro**-la). Words stressed on the last syllable (*Città*) always have a written accent.

text: In Italian phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Intonation}} is defined as: Italian has a melodic, undulating intonation pattern.
explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for Italian.
usage:
Rule: Intonation
Description: Italian has a melodic, undulating intonation pattern.
spanish: En la fonética de Italian, la regla de 'Intonation' es: Italian has a melodic, undulating intonation pattern.

text: What is the Italian pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Raddoppiamento Pro-Tip}}? To sound natural and fluent, you must practice the "Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico". When saying "va bene", join the words and hold the 'b' sound: "vahb-behneh". This is the hallmark of correct phonetic phrasing in Italian.
usage:
Tip: Raddoppiamento Pro-Tip
To sound natural and fluent, you must practice the "Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico". When saying "va bene", join the words and hold the 'b' sound: "vahb-behneh". This is the hallmark of correct phonetic phrasing in Italian.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Italian para 'Raddoppiamento Pro-Tip'? To sound natural and fluent, you must practice the "Raddoppiamento Fonosintattico". When saying "va bene", join the words and hold the 'b' sound: "vahb-behneh". This is the hallmark of correct phonetic phrasing in Italian.

text: What is the Italian pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Vowel Purity Rule}}? Never turn unstressed final vowels into the lazy English schwa sound. "Pizza" must end with a pure Spanish/Italian "ah" [a], never "pitz-uh" [ə].
usage:
Tip: Vowel Purity Rule
Never turn unstressed final vowels into the lazy English schwa sound. "Pizza" must end with a pure Spanish/Italian "ah" [a], never "pitz-uh" [ə].
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Italian para 'Vowel Purity Rule'? Never turn unstressed final vowels into the lazy English schwa sound. "Pizza" must end with a pure Spanish/Italian "ah" [a], never "pitz-uh" [ə].

text: What is the Italian pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Double Consonants}}? To pronounce double consonants correctly, hold the position of the consonant for a split second longer before releasing.
usage:
Tip: Double Consonants
To pronounce double consonants correctly, hold the position of the consonant for a split second longer before releasing.
spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de Italian para 'Double Consonants'? To pronounce double consonants correctly, hold the position of the consonant for a split second longer before releasing.
